source_metadata.title_raw: Alex J. Lorca - A-20-016 - February 13, 2020 - Monterey

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 13, 2020

Alex J. Lorca
City Attorney
City of Del Rey Oaks
Post Office Box 791
Monterey, CA 93941-0791

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-20-016

Dear Mr. Lorca:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Del Rey Oaks City ("City")
Councilmember Louise Goetzelt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Goetzelt participate in the City's threshold decision, to allow a north
side access road through the City to the Monterey Regional Airport, where her residence is located
within 500 feet of the preferred option for locating such a road?

CONCLUSION

No. It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision on the preferred option road will have a
material financial effect on her financial interest under Regulation 18702.2(a)(7); therefore,
Councilmember Goetzelt may not participate in the City's General Plan policy decision on whether
to allow any north access road.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-016
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Del Rey Oaks ("City") is adjacent to the Monterey Regional Airport ("Airport") and lies on its northeast side. The City's General Plan has a specific circulation element policy prohibition stating that the City will not support the potential north side access from Highway 218 and Del Rey Gardens Drive or any other airport access through the City for purposes of emergency access and access to and from other land uses subject to the approved Airport Master Plan. This prohibition has been in place for approximately 15 to 20 years. The City has a population of 1,650 and is primarily a residential community.

In November 2018, the Airport District approved a new Airport Master Plan. This new plan calls for development of the Airport's northeast side and includes a proposed north side access road to allow general public access to the north side of the Airport. The Airport District has considered seven north access road options, at least four of which would run through the small City. The Airport District has identified Del Gardens Drive, "Option 3," as the preferred north side access point and will carry this as the recommended location moving forward.² The Airport Master Plan notes this preference came after extensive review and input from Caltrans and other agencies. The Airport Master Plan states:

This road extends from a three-way intersection with Highway 218 then turns sharply to the north to provide access to a condominium community. At the turn, the road is adjacent to Airport property where the entrance road is considered. This potential access point would emanate from within the City of Del Rey Oaks. At the connection point with Airport property the terrain is very steep, which may require significant earthworks to maintain an appropriate road grade.

The Airport District requests that the City amend its the General Plan policy to allow a potential north side access. Currently, the City is considering an amendment to the policy.

Councilmember Goetzelt owns a condominium in the complex just off Del Gardens Drive. Her unit is within 500 feet of the proposed extension of Del Gardens Drive to access the Airport. By phone, Councilmember Goetzelt indicated that the access road will bring fire station access for the Airport fire station to service the City. Immediate changes in traffic are not anticipated, as the north side of the Airport is currently composed of private hangers; however, it is anticipated that the north access road will open up future north side Airport development.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on

² See AIRPORT MASTER PLAN-Monterey Regional Airport, Final, pages 5-28-29, Exhibit %T, and pages 6-20-22, http://monterey.airportstudy.com/files/2020/01/MRY-Volume-1-Final-012420-r_Part5.pdf, as viewed February 4, 2020.

File No. A-20-016
Page No. 3

the public generally, on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation
18700(a).) Section 87103 defines a financial interest to include any real property in which the
public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. Councilmember Goetzelt has a
financial interest in her condominium residence.

Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility, more than hypothetical or
theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Councilmember Goetzelt's real property interests are within the
City's General Plan; however, her real property is not the subject of the policy amendment decision.
(Regulation 18701(a).) Under Regulation 18701(b), she will have a financial interest in the decision
if it is a realistic possibility the decisions will have a material financial effect on her real property
interest.

Pertinent to the facts provided, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental
decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial ownership interest is
material whenever the governmental decision involves property located 500 feet or less from the
property line of the parcel, unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not
have any measurable impact on the official's property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).)

The decision at issue is whether to keep or end the City's policy prohibiting a north side
access road to the Airport. Ending the policy would potentially allow access to the Airport through
the City. The Airport District's preferred option for such a road would run within 500 feet of
Councilmember Goetzelt's residence. It is reasonably foreseeable that allowing any north side
access road through the City may result in an access to the Airport constructed within 500 feet of
her residence. It is reasonably foreseeable that this access road will have a financial effect on her
property. No clear and convincing evidence has been presented indicating that the decision would
have "no measurable impact" on the official's property.³ Accordingly, Councilmember Goetzelt has
a disqualifying financial interest under the Act.

Public Generally

A public official that has a reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in a
decision may still participate if the official demonstrates that the financial effect is (1) not unique to
the official and (2) shared by a significant segment of the jurisdiction.⁴ (Section 87103, Regulation
18703.) Relevant to these facts, Regulation 18703 defines a unique effect on the official's financial
interest(s) to include a disproportionate effect on either of the following:

(1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity.

___________________________
³ In addition to the materiality standard in Regulation 18702.2(a)(7), we note a finding of materiality may
also be appropriate in the event of a "Del Gardens Drive" access decision under Regulation 18702.2(a)(6), involving the
construction or improvement of streets, if the parcel will receive new or improved services that provide a benefit or
detriment disproportionate to other properties receiving the services.

⁴ A significant segment of the public includes at least 25 percent of all real property or residential real
property; or all individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).)

File No. A-20-016
Page No. 4

(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity of a
project that is the subject of a decision.

Given that there are several potential options for an access road through the small City of
1650 residents, the decision to allow any north access road through the City may impact additional
residential parcels. However, it appears that Councilmember Goetzelt's residential real property
interest will be uniquely affected due to its close proximity to the Airport District's preferred option
for the north access road to be considered if the City policy is amended. Due to this unique effect,
the public generally exception is not applicable here.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: L. Karen Harrison
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja
